title: Liquidity Raid Vs Breakout Acceptance
focus_questions: Did the sweep actually gain outside acceptance, or did it only run stops?
After the first burst, was the old boundary held outside or accepted back inside?

summary.thesis: A sweep through an obvious edge is only a liquidity raid until the market proves it can hold and build outside the old boundary.
summary.operator_translation: Do not chase the first stop run and do not fade it early; wait for outside hold quality to choose the branch.
summary.ai_usage_note: Use this card when a move through a key edge looks dramatic, but the real question is whether outside trade is accepted or quickly rejected.
summary.ai_brief: Environment: range edge, value edge, gap edge, or defended zone where stops can be swept. Required evidence: a burst through the edge plus proof of either outside hold or fast return inside. Do not trade: by chasing the first sweep or by fading it before outside hold fails. Real trigger: accepted outside hold for continuation or accepted return inside for raid failure. Failure point: the market proves the opposite branch by holding where it should have failed.

when_relevant: A visible auction edge or defended zone is swept.
The first burst looks strong enough to invite breakout chasing or emotional fading.
The operator needs to know whether the move is a real breakout or only a raid.
required_evidence: a meaningful edge was actually swept
the burst through the edge can be reviewed separately from later hold quality
outside trade either builds and holds or quickly returns back inside
confirmation_signals: the sweep pushes through a meaningful edge and forces obvious stop release
outside price either builds and holds, or quickly stalls and returns inside
the first pullback after the sweep reveals whether fresh control exists or not
invalidation_signals: the edge was not meaningful enough to matter
outside hold quality is still unresolved and the card is being forced too early
the market clearly proves the opposite branch after the first pullback
no_trade_conditions: Do not chase the first sweep just because it ran stops beyond a visible boundary.
Do not fade the sweep before outside hold quality actually fails.
Do not call breakout confirmed if price has not yet held and built outside the old area.

entry_archetypes.name: held outside retest
entry_archetypes.description: Enter after the sweep holds outside and the first retest proves the new auction is accepted.
entry_archetypes.risk_note: Avoid if outside hold is still thin and unproven.

entry_archetypes.name: failed sweep re-entry
entry_archetypes.description: Enter after the sweep fails and price is accepted back inside the old auction.
entry_archetypes.risk_note: Avoid if the return inside is only a brief flicker and not true re-acceptance.

management_notes: The first burst is often the worst location because it mixes information with emotion.
The best read usually comes after outside hold or outside failure is proven.
If price returns and accepts back inside the old auction, downgrade the breakout story quickly.
review_questions: Was the operator trading the sweep itself instead of the hold quality after the sweep?
Did outside trade become accepted, or was it only a stop run?
Was the reversal or continuation thesis formed before the market proved which branch it had chosen?
